cue: How do you say someone lost heart?

es.text: Caérsele a uno las alas del corazón
es.literal: for the wings of one's heart to fall off
es.meaning: To lose heart; to become discouraged.
es.example: Tras seis meses buscando trabajo se le cayeron las alas del corazón.
es.exampleTranslation: After six months job-hunting he lost heart.
es.regionNote: Literary-leaning; reviewer to say whether it is current speech in Mexico or mostly written.

it.meaning: To be dismayed; to lose all impetus.
it.example: Dopo sei mesi di ricerche gli sono cadute le braccia.
it.exampleTranslation: After six months of searching his arms just dropped.

bridge.note: Discouragement as a body part giving way, and the choice of part is the whole difference. Spanish drops the WINGS OF THE HEART — an organ that had been flying and now cannot, romantic and slightly literary. Italian drops the ARMS — the plain machinery of doing things, which simply stops. One language loses its lift, the other loses its grip.

interference.es_to_it: Wings and hearts are not the Italian image; there the arms fall — and note it takes essere: gli SONO cadute le braccia.
interference.it_to_es: 'Caérsele los brazos' is understood but is not the fixed Spanish phrase; the heart's wings do the falling.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh here). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Lift-vs-grip framing; ES side may be more literary than spoken — flagged.
